events.text: A mesh sack snagged on a ladder rung, heavy with honest salvage: connector housings, a coil of cable, somebody's lunchbox from a decade nobody misses. The scrapman upstairs pays without questions.
A crate wedged under the walkway, seals still good. The manifest sticker dissolved years ago, which legally makes it yours.

The tide shoulders through a bulkhead seam without warning — knee-deep goes waist-deep and the cold walks straight through your clothes and into your schedule. You find the ladder. You find nothing else.
Dark water, patient and empty. Your light crosses a school of something small and silver that has learned to live in a drowned hallway, which is more than most manage. Not every walk pays in credits.

Wedged in a valve wheel: a fist of sea-smoothed glass with a slow light moving inside it, patient as a heartbeat. Nobody upstairs can tell you what it is. Nobody offers to take it off your hands twice.

Far down the flooded gallery, past where your light gives up, someone is walking the water line — unhurried, exact, like the tide keeps their appointment book. They don't look your way. The dark takes them back a step at a time.
